title: Knowledge Check: Tobacco
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 15: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

A reminder before you begin: this is a history and policy class, not an endorsement of use. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA national helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours at 1-800-662-4357, and the quit-smoking line is 1-800-QUIT-NOW.

When you finish, take the Project assignment, where the bigger questions, how geography shaped slavery and how a deadly product was sold for a century, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What single idea does tobacco prove, opening Season 3?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"Legality and safety are not the same thing"},{"id":"q1b","text":"All legal products are safe"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Illegal products are always more harmful"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Tobacco is harmless"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Tobacco is legal almost everywhere yet kills more than 8 million people a year, proving that whether something is legal says little about whether it is safe.","citation":"Lesson: Sacred Smoke (intro)"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"What does tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) need to grow?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"A warm, humid season and light, well-drained sandy soil"},{"id":"q2b","text":"Frost and snow"},{"id":"q2c","text":"Pure desert sand"},{"id":"q2d","text":"Flooded paddies"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Tobacco needs about 100 to 130 frost-free days, warm temperatures, moderate rain, and light, slightly acidic sandy loam for the leaf chemistry the industry wants.","citation":"Lesson: The Tobacco Belt"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Why is tobacco production shifting toward countries like Malawi and Tanzania?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"As wealthy nations regulated tobacco, the industry moved toward places with less regulatory power"},{"id":"q3b","text":"Those countries banned all other crops"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Tobacco only grows there now"},{"id":"q3d","text":"Demand rose in wealthy nations"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"As regulation rose and demand fell in wealthy nations, the industry shifted growing and marketing toward lower-income countries, following the path of least regulation.","citation":"Lesson: The Tobacco Belt"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"Why do the American Tobacco Belt and the plantation South overlap so precisely?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"The same soil and climate that suited tobacco also suited the plantation labor model"},{"id":"q4b","text":"It is a pure coincidence"},{"id":"q4c","text":"Tobacco was grown only in the North"},{"id":"q4d","text":"Plantations avoided tobacco land"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Climate set the crop, the labor-punishing crop set the demand for labor, and that demand drove the expansion of slavery in the same region.","citation":"Lesson: The Belt and the Plantation"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"What was the documented chain linking tobacco to slavery?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Enslaved labor produced tobacco, tobacco profits bought more enslaved people, and slavery expanded"},{"id":"q5b","text":"Tobacco ended slavery"},{"id":"q5c","text":"There was no connection"},{"id":"q5d","text":"Tobacco was grown only by free workers"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Enslaved Africans arrived in Virginia from 1619 as tobacco became the main export, and the profits funded the purchase of more enslaved people, a self-reinforcing cycle.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacred to Commodity"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"What are cigarette filters actually made of?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Cellulose acetate, a plastic that does not biodegrade"},{"id":"q6b","text":"Biodegradable cotton"},{"id":"q6c","text":"Paper only"},{"id":"q6d","text":"Tobacco leaf"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Filters are cellulose acetate plastic, not cotton, and the roughly 4.5 trillion discarded each year are the single most collected item in coastal cleanups.","citation":"Lesson: The Footprint Beyond the Leaf"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"How much deforestation does tobacco curing drive each year?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"About 200,000 hectares, from burning wood to dry the leaf"},{"id":"q7b","text":"None at all"},{"id":"q7c","text":"It plants new forests"},{"id":"q7d","text":"About 10 hectares"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Curing tobacco with wood fires drives an estimated 200,000 hectares of deforestation a year, since curing one kilogram of tobacco takes about 7.8 kilograms of wood.","citation":"Lesson: The Footprint Beyond the Leaf"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"How did Indigenous peoples use tobacco before colonization?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"Ceremonially, as a sacred spiritual medicine, not recreationally"},{"id":"q8b","text":"Only as a snack food"},{"id":"q8c","text":"They did not use it"},{"id":"q8d","text":"As a building material"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Across the Americas, tobacco was used ceremonially as a sacred medicine for thousands of years, governed by spiritual protocol, not as a recreational product.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Pipe"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What is the Lakota chanunpa?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"The sacred pipe, tobacco held within a binding spiritual covenant"},{"id":"q9b","text":"A cigarette brand"},{"id":"q9c","text":"A tobacco tax"},{"id":"q9d","text":"A curing method"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"The chanunpa is the Lakota sacred pipe, one example of tobacco used within a spiritual covenant, a fundamentally different relationship to the plant than commercial tobacco.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Pipe"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"How did Indigenous tobacco governance differ from modern commercial tobacco?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"Ceremonial traditions governed who could use it, when, and how, with community accountability; commercial tobacco has only an age check"},{"id":"q10b","text":"There was no difference"},{"id":"q10c","text":"Indigenous use had no rules"},{"id":"q10d","text":"Commercial tobacco is more tightly governed"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"Sacred systems embedded tobacco in spiritual and community accountability, while modern commercial tobacco's only rule is an age check, a far looser form of governance.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Pipe"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What did the 1881 Bonsack cigarette machine enable?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Mass production of cigarettes, and with it mass addiction"},{"id":"q11b","text":"The end of tobacco"},{"id":"q11c","text":"A cure for cancer"},{"id":"q11d","text":"Hand-rolling only"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"The Bonsack machine let cigarettes be produced cheaply at huge scale, which turned an occasional product into a source of mass addiction.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacred to Commodity"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"What did the 1969 'doubt is our product' memo reveal about the tobacco industry?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"A deliberate strategy to manufacture public uncertainty to compete with established facts about harm"},{"id":"q12b","text":"That the industry promoted the science"},{"id":"q12c","text":"That cigarettes are safe"},{"id":"q12d","text":"That there were no internal documents"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"The Brown and Williamson memo named doubt itself as the product, exposing a deliberate effort to create public uncertainty against the known facts about cancer.","citation":"Lesson: Doubt Is Our Product"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"When did the tobacco industry's internal documents confirm cigarettes cause cancer?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"By the 1950s, while companies publicly denied it for decades"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Only in 2020"},{"id":"q13c","text":"Never"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Before tobacco existed"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Internal documents from the 1950s confirmed the cancer link, yet the companies publicly denied it into the 1990s, a documented case of corporate deception.","citation":"Lesson: Doubt Is Our Product"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What was the first official U.S. government acknowledgment of smoking's health risks?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"The 1964 Surgeon General's report on smoking"},{"id":"q14b","text":"The 1998 Master Settlement Agreement"},{"id":"q14c","text":"A 2024 fact sheet"},{"id":"q14d","text":"There was never one"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"The 1964 Surgeon General's report was the first official U.S. government acknowledgment that smoking causes serious health harm.","citation":"Lesson: Doubt Is Our Product"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"What is the trillion-dollar contradiction of tobacco?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"The industry earns about 800 billion dollars a year while the world spends about 1.4 trillion on the consequences"},{"id":"q15b","text":"Tobacco earns nothing"},{"id":"q15c","text":"The costs are smaller than the revenue"},{"id":"q15d","text":"There is no cost to society"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Tobacco generates roughly 800 billion dollars in annual revenue but costs the world about 1.4 trillion, leaving a net societal cost on the order of 600 billion a year.","citation":"Lesson: The Trillion-Dollar Contradiction"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"What is a negative externality, using tobacco as the example?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"A cost created by a producer but paid by others, here profitable for shareholders and costly for everyone else"},{"id":"q16b","text":"A benefit paid by the producer"},{"id":"q16c","text":"A tax on imports"},{"id":"q16d","text":"A type of subsidy"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"A negative externality is a cost a producer creates but does not pay; tobacco's harms fall on smokers, families, and health systems while profits go to shareholders.","citation":"Lesson: The Trillion-Dollar Contradiction"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"Where do most smokers live, according to the WHO?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"About 80 percent of the world's 1.3 billion smokers live in low- and middle-income countries"},{"id":"q17b","text":"Almost all live in wealthy nations"},{"id":"q17c","text":"There are very few smokers left"},{"id":"q17d","text":"Only in the United States"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Of about 1.3 billion smokers worldwide, roughly 80 percent live in low- and middle-income countries, where the harm increasingly concentrates.","citation":"Lesson: The Trillion-Dollar Contradiction"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What is Green Tobacco Sickness?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"Nicotine poisoning absorbed through the skin from handling wet tobacco leaves"},{"id":"q18b","text":"A plant disease"},{"id":"q18c","text":"A marketing term"},{"id":"q18d","text":"A tax on green tobacco"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Farmworkers who handle wet tobacco leaves can absorb nicotine through the skin and suffer poisoning, one of several hazards of low-paid tobacco labor.","citation":"Lesson: Who Pays for a Cigarette"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"About what share of tobacco value do smallholder farmers capture?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Only about 5 to 10 percent, the bottom of the smile curve"},{"id":"q19b","text":"About 90 percent"},{"id":"q19c","text":"All of it"},{"id":"q19d","text":"Exactly half"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Farmers capture only about 5 to 10 percent, while manufacturers take 30 to 40 percent and distribution, retail, and taxes take the rest, the same smile curve seen all series.","citation":"Lesson: Who Pays for a Cigarette"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"What was the 1998 Master Settlement Agreement?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"A 206 billion dollar settlement between 46 states and major tobacco companies, the largest civil litigation settlement in U.S. history"},{"id":"q20b","text":"A ban on all tobacco"},{"id":"q20c","text":"A subsidy for tobacco farmers"},{"id":"q20d","text":"An international treaty"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"In 1998, 46 states settled lawsuits against the major tobacco companies for 206 billion dollars over 25 years, with funds meant for prevention and cessation.","citation":"Lesson: The Settlement That Wasn't"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What actually happened to most Master Settlement Agreement money?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Many states diverted it to general budgets, spending less than 3 percent on tobacco prevention"},{"id":"q21b","text":"All of it funded prevention"},{"id":"q21c","text":"It was returned to the companies"},{"id":"q21d","text":"It was never paid"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Despite receiving about 27 billion dollars a year from the settlement and tobacco taxes, states spent under 3 percent on prevention, showing settlements do not auto-redirect funds.","citation":"Lesson: The Settlement That Wasn't"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What did Edward Bernays do in 1929 with 'Torches of Freedom'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Staged women lighting cigarettes at a parade, tying smoking to women's liberation, and got the press to cover it as news"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Banned cigarette advertising"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Invented the filter"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Exposed the cancer link"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Bernays staged a New York event framing cigarettes as 'torches of freedom' for women, and the press reported the promotion as if it were genuine news.","citation":"Lesson: Torches of Freedom"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What is rhetorical appropriation, as in 'Torches of Freedom'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Borrowing the moral weight of one cause, like feminism, to serve another, like selling an addictive product"},{"id":"q23b","text":"Banning a cause"},{"id":"q23c","text":"Telling the literal truth"},{"id":"q23d","text":"A type of tax"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Appropriation here means attaching the moral authority of women's liberation to a cigarette campaign, disguising a sales pitch as a social movement.","citation":"Lesson: Torches of Freedom"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What rhetorical appeal did 'More Doctors Smoke Camels' ads use?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Ethos, the appeal to authority, using medical endorsement to sell cigarettes"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Pure logic with real data"},{"id":"q24c","text":"No appeal at all"},{"id":"q24d","text":"An appeal to fear of doctors"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"By implying doctors endorsed the brand, the ads borrowed medical authority (ethos) to reassure smokers, even as the science showed serious harm.","citation":"Lesson: The Language of Doubt"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What is euphemism, as used in tobacco marketing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Softer language replacing direct description, like calling cigarettes 'light' or 'mild'"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Harsh, blunt language"},{"id":"q25c","text":"A scientific measurement"},{"id":"q25d","text":"A type of filter"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Words like 'light' and 'mild' softened a deadly product's image, which is why regulators eventually restricted those descriptors.","citation":"Lesson: The Language of Doubt"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"What is strategic ambiguity?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"Language that is technically accurate but functionally misleading"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Clear, honest language"},{"id":"q26c","text":"A random choice of words"},{"id":"q26d","text":"A legal contract"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Strategic ambiguity uses statements that are literally defensible but designed to mislead, the heart of the industry's decades-long doubt strategy.","citation":"Lesson: The Language of Doubt"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What does the slavery-tobacco connection parallel from Season 2?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"The rum-and-slavery connection of the Triangular Trade in Episode 11"},{"id":"q27b","text":"The Japanese whisky story"},{"id":"q27c","text":"The French wine AOC"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Nothing in Season 2"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Like rum, tobacco was a commodity built on enslaved labor whose profits financed more enslavement, the same extraction logic across both episodes.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacred to Commodity"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"Where does tobacco fit in the Indiana geography of this episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Southwestern Indiana counties share the belt's climate and once grew burley tobacco"},{"id":"q28b","text":"Indiana never grew tobacco"},{"id":"q28c","text":"All of Indiana is the tobacco belt"},{"id":"q28d","text":"Indiana is the world's largest producer"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Vanderburgh, Warrick, and Gibson counties in southwestern Indiana share enough of the belt's climate to grow burley tobacco, now much declined.","citation":"Lesson: The Tobacco Belt"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What did Jean Nicot do in 1560?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Sent tobacco to the French court as a medicine; nicotine was later named after him"},{"id":"q29b","text":"Banned tobacco in France"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Discovered the cancer link"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Invented the cigarette machine"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Nicot promoted tobacco to the French court as a medicine, and the compound nicotine was named after him, an early reframing of the plant.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacred to Commodity"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What was the 2003 WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"The first international public-health treaty"},{"id":"q30b","text":"A tobacco subsidy program"},{"id":"q30c","text":"A cigarette brand"},{"id":"q30d","text":"A U.S. state law"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"The 2003 WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control was the first international public-health treaty, a coordinated global response to tobacco harm.","citation":"Lesson: Doubt Is Our Product"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"Why is tobacco called a bridge into Season 3?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"It is legal yet deadly, showing that the line between legal and illegal is drawn by history and power, not just danger"},{"id":"q31b","text":"It is the only safe substance"},{"id":"q31c","text":"It connects to nothing else"},{"id":"q31d","text":"It is illegal everywhere"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Tobacco sits between Season 2's legal drinks and Season 3's controlled substances, proving legality reflects history, money, and power more than pure danger.","citation":"Lesson: Sacred Smoke (intro)"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What does tobacco's filter waste reveal about its full footprint?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"The harm reaches the environment too, with about 4.5 trillion plastic filters polluting land and water yearly"},{"id":"q32b","text":"Filters fully biodegrade"},{"id":"q32c","text":"There is no waste"},{"id":"q32d","text":"Filters are recycled into food"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Cigarette filters are the most collected item in coastal cleanups, so tobacco's harm extends from the smoker's lungs to the world's beaches and waterways.","citation":"Lesson: The Footprint Beyond the Leaf"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What was Edward Bernays known for beyond the cigarette campaign?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"Pioneering public relations and writing the book Propaganda about shaping public opinion"},{"id":"q33b","text":"Discovering nicotine"},{"id":"q33c","text":"Running a tobacco farm"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Inventing the filter"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Bernays was a founder of modern public relations and wrote Propaganda (1928), arguing that public opinion could be deliberately shaped, which his cigarette work demonstrated.","citation":"Lesson: Torches of Freedom"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What is the core media-literacy lesson of the tobacco episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Learning to read the gap between what corporate language says and what it hides"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Believing every advertisement"},{"id":"q34c","text":"Ignoring all marketing"},{"id":"q34d","text":"That language never misleads"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"From staged news to medical endorsements to the doubt strategy, the episode teaches readers to spot the distance between corporate claims and the truth they conceal.","citation":"Lesson: The Language of Doubt"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"How does tobacco's modern production shift echo a Season 2 pattern?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"It follows regulatory arbitrage, moving toward places with less power to regulate, like corporations seeking weaker rules"},{"id":"q35b","text":"It moves toward the strictest regulators"},{"id":"q35c","text":"It has no pattern"},{"id":"q35d","text":"It only grows in wealthy nations now"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"Just as some producers seek the weakest oversight, tobacco's growing and marketing shifted toward lower-income countries with less regulatory power.","citation":"Lesson: The Tobacco Belt"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, what resource does this season give?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"The SAMHSA national helpline, free and confidential 24/7 at 1-800-662-4357"},{"id":"q36b","text":"No resources are available"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Only a website"},{"id":"q36d","text":"A tobacco company hotline"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Every Season 3 episode points to the SAMHSA national helpline, 1-800-662-4357, free, confidential, and open 24 hours; to quit tobacco, call 1-800-QUIT-NOW.","citation":"Lesson: Sacred Smoke (intro)"}]}
